coredata.dc:title: An overview of recent advances in quantum dots for biomedical applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: He, Xuewen
Ma, Nan
coredata.dc:description: During the past ten years significant advances have been achieved in quantum dot (QD) research field. The new synthetic methods and the discovery of new types of QDs have enabled a variety of new applications of QDs for bioimaging and biosensing. This review will focus on the most recent progress of QDs for biomedical applications. Ample examples will be given in this review on newly developed synthetic methods of QDs, non-toxic QDs, QDs for biomolecule detection, cell and animal imaging, and disease therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dot
Synthesis
Biosensing
Bioimaging
Therapy

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: New types of QDs for biological applications

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Aqueous synthesis of CdTe QDs using glutathione as ligands. Image of different-sized GSH-coated CdTe nanocrystals illuminated under an ultraviolet lamp (top) and the corresponding absorption (bottom left) and photoluminescence (bottom right) spectra.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [16]. Copyright 2006 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: DNA-programmed and functionalized CdTe nanocrystals: (a) design of chimeric oligonucleotides with ligand (ps) and recognition (po) domains and schematic representation of one-pot biofunctionalized nanocrystal synthesis; (b) binding to biological targets.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [25]. Copyright 2010 American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of QDs in yeast cells. (a) Route for unnatural biosynthesis of fluorescent CdSe quantum dots and (b) images for subcellular location of intracellular fluorescence.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [41]. Copyright 2009 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Multicolor Si QDs. Characterization of visible and NIR emitting Si QDs produced by etching of laser-synthesized Si nanoparticles. (a) Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of green-emitting Si QDs (top), orange-emitting Si QDs (middle), formaldehyde fixed micelle encapsulated Si QDs (bottom), (b) photograph, emission spectra of photostability of Vis-NIR emitting Si QDs excited by a single source (365nm).

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [129]. Copyright 2011 American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cytosolic QD delivery for intracellular labeling. Specific labeling of actin filaments (A) and mitochondria (M) using intracellularly delivered QD-antibody conjugates. Nucleus (N) is stained with DAPI.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [180]. Copyright 2011 American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: NIR-II fluorescence imaging of a xenograft tumor with high uptake of 6PEG-Ag2S QDs. Time course of NIR-II fluorescence images and white-light optical image of the mouse injected with 6PEG-Ag2S QDs.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [193]. Copyright 2012 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FRET-based QD probe for protease detection. (a) Schematic of the QD-fluorescent protein sensor. mCherry with an N-terminal linker expressing the caspase 3 cleavage site and a His6 sequence were self-assembled to the surface of CdSe-ZnS DHLA QDs, resulting in FRET quenching of the QD and sensitized emission from the mCherry acceptor. Caspase 3 cleaves the linker, reducing the FRET efficiency. (b) Linker sequences. The original 35 residue N-terminal linker is shown with colors highlighting functionalities, including the start methionine (Met), the His6 NTA-purification/QD-assembly sequence, several other functionalities, and the first residues of the mCherry protein. Caspase 3 cleavage site insertions into the linkers are highlighted in yellow. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [226]. Copyright 2009 American Chemical Society.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: QDs-based siRNA delivery. Rational design of proton-sponge coated quantum dots and their use as a multifunctional nanoscale carrier for siRNA delivery and intracellular imaging. (a) Chemical modification of polymer-encapsulated QDs to introduce tertiary amine groups, and adsorption of siRNA on the particle surface by electrostatic interactions. (b) Schematic diagram showing the steps of siRNA-QD in membrane binding, cellular entry, endosomal escape, capturing by RNA binding proteins, loading to RNA-induced silencing complexes (RISC), target degradation and the proton-sponge effect showing the involvement of the membrane protein ATPase (proton pump), osmotic pressure buildup, and organelle swelling and rupture.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from Ref. [179]. Copyright 2008 American Chemical Society.

head.ce:title: An overview of recent advances in quantum dots for biomedical applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: During the past ten years significant advances have been achieved in quantum dot (QD) research field. The new synthetic methods and the discovery of new types of QDs have enabled a variety of new applications of QDs for bioimaging and biosensing. This review will focus on the most recent progress of QDs for biomedical applications. Ample examples will be given in this review on newly developed synthetic methods of QDs, non-toxic QDs, QDs for biomolecule detection, cell and animal imaging, and disease therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor nanocrystals with unique size dependent optical and electronic properties originated from quantum size effect [1]. QDs possess several advantages as lumiphores for bio-applications including high photostability, broad absorption spectra, large extinction coefficient, and tunable emission wavelengths [2]. Since QDs were introduced into the biomedical field in 1998 [3,4], this intriguing nanomaterial has attracted numerous attentions and the related research field has developed extremely fast over the past ten years. Recent research in this field has been focusing on developing less toxic QDs, constructing compact and biocompatible QDs with novel capping ligands, novel imaging and sensing techniques based on QDs, and multifunctional QD nanocomposites. This review intends to provide an overview of the most recent progress of QDs in the biomedical research field.

Bottom-up colloidal synthesis is the most common approach to synthesize highly dispersed QDs for bio-applications. Conventional QD synthesis developed 20 years ago relies on organometallic routes that are conducted in organic solvents under high temperature and inert environment [5]. The formation of colloidal QDs undergoes three steps – nucleation, growth, and termination. Sizes and emission wavelengths of QDs could be finely tuned by changing reaction conditions. These QDs exhibit high monodispersity, high quantum yields, and narrow emission peak which are superior for bioimaging applications. QDs that are composed of various elements such as cadmium (Cd)-, indium (In)-, and lead (Pb)-based QDs have been synthesized using this method [6–8]. However, these QDs are capped with hydrophobic molecules and need to be further solubilized in aqueous solution before practical use. The solubilization step can be achieved by either ligand exchange or encapsulation of QDs with amphipathic polymers, or further decorated by water-soluble silica shell [3,9–11]. Subsequently, the QDs could be conjugated with biomolecules for specific labeling or targeting, which could be achieved by covalent bioconjugation chemistry, biotin-avidin interaction, electrostatic interaction et al. [3,12,13]. As an alternative approach, aqueous solution-based synthesis has attracted more attentions recently due to its mild synthetic conditions, the elimination of the solubilization step, and the use of simple and cheap reaction instruments. Small thiol-containing molecules are the mostly used ligands for aqueous synthesis of QDs [14–16]. So far cadmium, silver, lead, indium-based QDs and the related core–shell, alloy, and doped QDs have been successfully synthesized using this method [11,17–22]. Similar to the organometallic route, the size and emission wavelength of QDs synthesized in aqueous solution could also be controlled by reaction conditions, making this method a good choice to generate water-soluble QDs for bio-applications (Fig. 1 ).

The biomineralization process occurred in nature has inspired people to mimic this process to generate inorganic materials using biomolecules templates and scaffolds. A variety of biomolecules such as nucleic acids, peptides, proteins, and polysaccharides have been used as template to synthesize QDs. Given the strong metal-chelating properties of nucleic acids molecules, they can serve as excellent template to initiate QD nucleation, modulate QD growth and finally passivate on QD surface [23–26]. Interestingly, the sequence and structure of nucleic acids are found to exert a big influence on the size, morphology and optical properties of the QDs [24,26]. These nucleic acids-capped QDs exhibit robust biocompatibility and colloidal stability that are well suited for cell imaging studies [27–30]. Recently, the DNA-templated QD synthesis was used to directly generate biofunctionalized QDs in one step using chimeric DNA molecules as templates (Fig. 2 ). The chimeric DNA contains a phosphorothioate domain that serves as the passivation domain for QD growth and a phosphate domain that serves as a biorecognition domain for biomolecular binding [28,31]. Further, the number of DNA molecules caped on each QD could be precisely tuned using this method. These DNA-capped QDs with different DNA valencies could be assembled to form higher-order nanostructures with various geometries [32]. Peptide molecules such as the naturally occurring glutathione molecules have also been used for QDs synthesis [33,34]. The cysteine group of the peptide is the critical moiety for QD passivation. The as-prepared QDs exhibit excellent size-dependent optical properties and good biocompatibility. Protein-directed QD synthesis was also widely explored. The synthesis needs to be conducted under very mild conditions to avoid protein denaturing, which somewhat compromises the quality and the luminescence intensity of the as-prepared QDs [33,35–40]. Not until very recently a general strategy to synthesize highly luminescent QDs using protein molecule as ligands was developed [33]. This synthesis can be completed in one second and the protein molecules capped on QDs remain their activities. The use of these protein-QD conjugates for biocatalysis and bioimaging was demonstrated.

Recently, the QD synthesis has been moved from test tubes to living organisms. Living cells such as yeast cells and E. coli cells could serve as reactors to mediate the formation of QDs following the introduction of QD precursors into the cells [41,42] (Fig. 3 ). The intracellular glutathione molecules or the genetically encoded QD binding peptide could serve as ligands to mediate the formation of the QDs in the cells [43]. The size and emission wavelength of QDs could be tuned by changing the duration of incubation time of yeast cells [42]. Near-infrared QDs could also be synthesized in this way and used for live animal imaging [44]. The QDs synthesis could be even performed in whole living organisms. For example, a recent study reports the synthesis of high quality CdTe QDs in earthworm [45]. These QDs were extracted and purified for cell imaging applications

There are a lot of other methods for QDs synthesis such as microwave-assisted synthesis [46], cation-exchange-based synthesis [47,48], seeded synthesis [49], polymer-templated synthesis [50], and top-down approach [51]. These methods could serve as alternatives to the conventional methods to prepare QDs with desired properties, especially for those difficult to be directly prepared via the traditional routes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: New types of QDs for biological applications

ce:section.ce:para: NIR QDs have been extensively used for living animal optical imaging such as tumor imaging, blood vessel imaging, and lymph node mapping. The first generation of NIR QDs are composed of cadmium, mercury, or lead [22,51–58]. To minimum the potential in vivo toxicity of these QDs, protection layers such as silica coating or non-toxic elements-based QD shell were necessary to prevent the release of the toxic components into the biological milieu. The second generation of QDs such as indium-based and silver-based QDs are believed to be less toxic and are currently more popular in animal imaging studies. Particularly, those NIR QDs emitting in the second biological window (1000–1400nm) are demonstrated to provide higher imaging resolution and depth than those emitting in the first biological window (700–900nm) [59–61]. The indium- and silver-based QDs could be synthesized either in organic solvent or in aqueous solution [17,19,62–70]. One disadvantage of these QDs is the relatively low QY, which may need to be further improved in the future. NIR-emitting QDs were also obtained from alloyed QDs [56,57,71], type II QDs [72], or core–shell QDs with lattice strain tuning [21,73].

Fluorescent metal nanoclusters (NCs) have gained increasing attentions recently owing to their high potentials for biological applications. These metal NCs possess strong photoluminescence and photostability that outperform the traditional organic fluorophores [74,75]. Additionally, the less toxicity and smaller size of these metal NCs make them more promising lumiphores than QDs for bioimaging applications [76–87]. The metal NCs are composed of several to a hundred atoms and have an overall size less than 2nm. Due to the very small sizes, these NCs display discrete and size-tunable electronic transitions resulted from the strong quantum confinement of free electrons, which leads to intense fluorescence. Biomolecule-templated synthesis is a major strategy to generate fluorescent metal NCs. For example, DNA molecules have been used to synthesize Ag and Cu NCs [79,88–102]. The fluorescence properties of the Ag and Cu NCs are highly dependent on the sequence and structure of DNA molecules [92,94,96,97,101,103]. This DNA-templated NCs synthesis has enabled the development of novel methods for the detection of DNA single mutations [90–92], microRNA [98], small molecules [82,88,89,99], and metal ions [100,102]. Protein and peptide molecules have also been used to synthesize fluorescent metal nanoclusters. For example, BSA was used to mediate the formation of red-emitting Au clusters [104]; peptides containing cysteine residues were used to synthesize Ag NCs [105]. Fluorescent Au NCs could serve as novel probes for sensing of intracellular hydroxyl radicals [77,84], temperatures [85], and glutathione [82]. In vivo imaging using Au NCs was also reported [78,80,81,86,87]. These small Au NCs are capable of passive tumor targeting and undergo efficient renal clearance [86]. Other than biomolecule templates, artificial polymers could also be used to synthesize fluorescent metal NCs. For example, poly(amidoamine) (PAMAM) and poly(methacrylic acid) (PMAA) were used to synthesize Au and Ag NCs [74,78,80,81,86,87,106]. In addition, small thiol ligands were also good candidates to synthesize Au and Ag NCs with ultrafine size [105,107].

Luminescent carbon dots are promising probes for bioimaging applications due to their low toxicity, chemical inertness, and versatile surface chemistry [108–110]. These CDs are usually produced by laser ablation of carbon in the presence of water and then surface passivated with polymers [111]. These CDs exhibit different emission colors when excited with different emission wavelengths. CDs have been applied to different bio-applications including cancer cell targeting [112], intracellular pH sensing [113,114], Cu2+ sensing [114] et al. Also, the biodistribution, clearance, and tumor uptake of CDs have been systematically explored for in vivo animal imaging [108]. Photosensitizer-conjugated CDs have been used for simultaneous fluorescence imaging and photodynamic therapy [115].

Graphene is monolayer type of carbon composed of a one-atom thick two-dimensional crystal of sp2 carbon atoms arranged in a honeycomb lattice. GQDs are usually obtained from graphite following a two-step protocol. The first step is to prepare graphene oxide (GO) from graphite powders using the Hummers method [116]. The second step is to cut the GO into small dots at high temperatures [117,118]. The as-prepared GQDs display good water solubility owing to carboxyl groups introduced during oxidation [119]. So far most GQDs prepared using this method exhibit blue or green photoluminescence. It is believed that the size and shape of GQDs have a dramatic impact on the photoluminescence properties of GQDs [120]. These GQDs could be easily functionalized with biomolecules for sensing and imaging applications. For example, insulin-conjugated GQDs could be used for real-time tracking of dynamics of insulin receptors in cells [121]. Polydopamine and hyaluronic acid-functionalized GQDs were used for cell and animal imaging [122,123]. The use of GQDs for protein kinase sensing was also demonstrated, which is based on the aggregation-induced photoluminescence quenching of GQDs [124]. The in vivo biodistribution and toxicology of carboxylated GQDs were investigated. No acute toxicity was detected both in vitro and in vivo [125].

Silicon dots hold great promise to bio-applications owing to their strong photoluminescence, high photostability, low toxicity, and tunable emission wavelength [126]. Several methods have been developed to synthesize luminescent silicon dots, including etching of porous Si, laser ablation, solution phase methods, low-pressur plasma synthesis, thermal processing of silesquioxane, and two-step method based on laser pyrolysis of silane followed by acid etching [127–129]. The latter one could produce Si QDs with high QY and tunable emission wavelength from 450nm to 800nm (Fig. 4 ). Si QDs could be conjugated with biorecognition molecules for specific cell targeting and labeling [126–128]. In vivo targeted cancer imaging [130], sentinel lymph node mapping [130] and multi-channel imaging with biocompatible Si QDs [130] have also been demonstrated.

One potential limitation of the traditional QDs for optical imaging is that they require a light excitation to illuminate light. The excitation light usually has a shorter wavelength with low tissue penetration capability. Also, the excitation light will cause tissue autofluorescence and reduce the signal-to-background ratio. To overcome this barrier, people developed the so-called self-illuminating QDs that do not require any light excitation to illuminate light. The first kind of self-illuminating QDs is based on bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET) [131,132]. In this construct luciferase is conjugated QDs and the QD emission can be induced by the addition of luciferin. These luciferease-QD conjugates were successfully applied to in vivo imaging with reduced imaging background [131,132]. Additionally, these BRET-based QDs were also used for detection of protease activities [133]. Similar to BRET, the chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (CLRET)-based QDs [134] and Cerenkov resonance energy transfer (CRET)-based QDs [135–137] were also reported. CRET-based QDs have been constructed by conjugating Cu64 to QDs and used for in vivo imaging studies [138].

Functionalization of QDs with biomolecules is a critical step to allow QDs to specifically recognize and bind with the cognate biomolecular targets. Numerous methods have been developed to conjugate biomolecules to QD surface. In general, the bioconjugation methods could be divided into covalent conjugation and non-covalent conjugation. Different chemical groups could be used for covalent crosslinking including carboxyl group, amine group, thiol group et al. [3,139,140]. In some cases a crosslinker is required to join two chemical groups together. Azide click chemistry has recently been applied for nanoparticle bioconjugation [141]. Non-covalent interactions such as electrostatic interaction, metal chelation, and biotin-avidin binding could also be used for QD bioconjugation. Electrostatic interaction could be used to attach negatively charged nucleic acids or protein molecules to positively charged QDs [13,142,143]. Alternatively, protein or peptides containing strong metal ion-chelating moieties could directly attach to the QD surface. For example, a polyhistidine tag fused to protein molecules could serve as a linker to conjugate proteins to QDs surface owing to the high affinity of histidine residues with zinc and nickel ions [144]. Also, as previously mentioned in the text, protein and DNA molecules could be directly used as ligands to synthesize biofunctionalized QDs [28,33]. In this case, the conjugation step is combined with the QD synthesis step. Biotin-avidin conjugation is another solution to attach biomolecules onto QDs [12,145,146].

Once the QDs are functionalized with biomolecules, they could be used for specific cell labeling and imaging. Because QDs have very strong photoluminescence and high photostability, they could offer much more intense and persistent signals than most organic fluorophores, enabling QD-based single-particle tracking in the cells [12,147]. Besides, QDs have been frequently used for multi-color imaging, through which multiple targets could be simultaneously detected and profiled [146,148–151]. So far QDs have been extensively applied to cell surface receptor labeling [145,152–158], intracellular biomolecule tracking and sensing [12,147,159–168], organelle targeting [169,170] et al. A variety of biomolecule ligands have been used to functionalize QDs for specific labeling. The most common ligands are proteins (antibodies), peptides, aptamers, and small biomolecules. They are widely used to specifically label cell surface proteins and receptors that are overexpressed on certain cell types. For example, prostate-specific membrane antigen, Her2, EGFR that are overexpressed on different cancer cells could be labeled with biofunctionalized QDs for cancer diagnostics [141,145,171]. Arginine-glycineaspartate (RGD) peptide which binds to cell surface integrin is also a popular ligand for cancer cell targeting [172–174]. Nucleic acids aptamers are generally identified from a large DNA/RNA library through in vitro selection using either cell surface proteins or whole cells as targets [175]. These aptamers possess very high affinity with their targets. Compared to antibodies, aptamers are usually more stable, easier to synthesize and manipulate. RNA and DNA aptamers have been used to target different cell types including prostate cancer cells, leukemia cells, and lymphoma cells et al. [28,176,177]. Small molecules such as folate acids are also a good choice for cell targeting. Folic acid receptors overexpressed on a variety of cancer cells could be imaged with folate acid-capped QDs [178–180]. The use of these small molecule ligands could help to minimize the overall hydrodynamic size of the bioconjugated QDs, which is a critical parameter for in vivo imaging applications.

While cell surface labeling with QDs could be readily achieved, specific intracellular labeling with QDs in live cells is much more challenging and so far has not been fully resolved. The QDs are relatively big and could not directly penetrate cell membranes like small organic fluorophores. Instead, the QDs are internalized into the cells via a different mechanism called endocytosis. Endocytosis could be either non-specific or specific (e.g. receptor-mediated endocytosis). The QDs are finally trapped into late endosomes and lysosomes [3,146,181]. These compartments are isolated from most biomolecules in cytoplasm and therefore hamper the intracellular biomolecule labeling with QDs. Early attempts for QDs cytosolic delivery are done by conjugating QDs with cell penetrating peptides (CPP) or using liposome-based transfection agents. However, significant QDs aggregation and endosomal sequestration were observed for these two methods [181–184]. So far a few strategies have been developed to overcome this barrier to achieve cytosolic delivery of QDs. PEI-based endosomal disrupting polymers were capped on QD surface and help the QDs to escape from endosome and lysosome. These polymers could absorb a lot of protons in the acidic compartments and build up an osmotic pressure across the membrane which will finally disrupt the membrane and allow the QDs to enter cytosol [185,186]. One limitation of this approach is the pronounced toxicity caused by the polymers. Another method for cytosolic QDs delivery is based on biodegradable polymeric nanospheres [187]. The QDs encapsulated in those nanospheres were delivered into cell cytosol following nanosphere degradation and then targeted specific cell compartments (Fig. 5 ). Nonetheless, microinjection is still the most efficient way to deliver QDs into cell cytosol [9,150,183], although it is quite tedious and low throughput. Alternatively, the cells could be fixed before imaging studies. This method is more suitable for quantitative identification of specific intracellular biomolecules. Recent progress of QD-based cell imaging includes multiplex protein tracking, monitoring intracellular protein interaction dynamics, stem cell labeling and imaging, gene expression detection et al. [12,147,159–168,175]. Especially, as a bright and robust fluorophore, QD is quite useful for single molecule imaging [188,189]. It opens up the possibility to monitor complex biological process in real-time [163,190].

Cytotoxicity is a major concern for QD imaging studies. The QD cytotoxicity mainly originates from the release of toxic metal ions (e.g. Cd2+ ions) from the QD surface. Enhancing the colloidal stability using multidentate capping molecules or growing a non-toxic inorganic shell on the surface of the QD core could prevent the release of heavy metal ions[191,192]. In addition, QDs are known to generate free radicals that would cause oxidative stress in cells [193,194]. QD capping ligands are another reason to cause toxicity [195]. Highly inert and biocompatible molecules are preferred to minimize potential cytotoxicity[196,197]. Alternatively, non-toxic elements-based QDs as mentioned in the second section could be used for cell imaging studies [198–201].

Traditional organic fluorophores (e.g. ICG) for NIR fluorescence imaging are limited by their low QYs and low photostability. Highly luminescent NIR QDs have found tremendous use for in vivo imaging applications including tumor targeting, lymph node mapping, blood vessel imaging et al. [149,202,203]. The NIR light has better tissue penetration capability than visible light and could be detected in deep tissue (up to ∼1cm) [202]. During the past decade QDs has become a very popular tool for in vivo cancer detection using xenografted tumor models of small animals [149,174]. In vivo tumor targeting is based on two main strategies – passive tumor targeting and active tumor targeting [149]. The endothelium (innermost cell layer) of tumor capillaries is less well organized and have more numerous and larger pores than normal healthy vessels. QDs with a suitable size stay in the blood stream until reaching tumor tissue where they selectively leak into the tumor tissue. This gives a larger difference in concentration of the QDs in tumor tissue compared to normal tissue. Active tumor targeting refers to specific binding with cancer cells surface in the tumor site with biofunctionalized QDs. Compared with passive tumor targeting, active tumor targeting is more efficient and durable and could offer higher imaging contrast [174]. Moreover, the active tumor targeting is necessary when discrimination of specific tumor types is required.

Several considerations need to be taken into account for designing QD probes for in vivo tumor imaging. First of all, QD biocompatibility is a critical parameter to affect the in vivo behavior of QDs. QDs with low biocompatibility could absorb a lot of serum proteins and then rapidly recognized and cleared by the reticuloendothelial system (RES) and finally accumulated in liver and spleen [204]. To ensure a high tumor targeting efficiency and minimum imaging background, the biocompatibility of the QDs should be optimized to increase their retention time in blood and prevent the accumulation of QDs in non-specific organs. So far two types of ligands molecules have been widely used to generate highly biocompatible QDs. One is polyethylene glycol (PEG) molecule and the other one is zwitterionic molecule [205–208]. PEG molecules are long neutral polymers that are highly resistant to non-specific protein absorption. These molecules could be conjugated to QDs surface to prevent RES clearance. Zwitterionic molecules contain an equal number of positive and negative charges and the overall charge is zero. These molecules could also avoid non-specific absorption [145,208]. An additional advantage of zwitterionic molecules is that they are much smaller than PEG molecules and would not significantly increase the hydrodynamic size of the QDs after modification [209]. Secondly, the size of QDs plays a critical role in QD biodistribution. It is known that QDs accumulation in normal tissues and organs would cause potential long-term toxicity and may hamper their clinical use. Therefore, efficient clearance of QDs from the body in an appropriate time duration is highly preferred. Renal clearance is the most efficient way to clear nanoparticles from the body. The efficiency of nanoparticle renal clearance is highly dependent on their hydrodynamic sizes. A hydrodynamic diameter <5.5nm resulted in rapid and efficient urinary excretion and elimination of quantum dots from the body [209]. However, such small HD is not easy to achieve for the biofunctionalized QDs. The best solution to construct QDs with both small HD and targeting capability is to use small zwitterionic molecule as capping ligands and small biorecognition molecules (e.g. folic acid) for biotargeting [174,210]. In addition, multidentate polymeric cappling ligands could also offer compact QDs with minimized HD sizes and robust colloidal stabilities [211–213]. Recently, simultaneous highly efficient QD-based tumor targeting and renal clearance have been achieved [174,197]. Another important in vivo application of QDs is lymph node imaging. NIR QDs permit sentinel lymph nodes 1cm deep to be imaged easily in real time [202]. It provides the surgeon with direct visual guidance throughout the entire SLN mapping procedure, minimize incision and dissection inaccuracies, and permit real-time confirmation of complete resection. In addition, different types of lymph nodes could be simultaneously imaged using QDs with different emission wavelengths [148].

Recently QDs for in vivo imaging was realized in the second biological window (1000–1400nm) [59–61,200,214]. Compared to the first biological window (700–900nm), fluorescence imaging in the second biological window is more desirable owing to reduced photon scattering, deeper tissue penetration, and lower autofluorescence. NIR Ag2S QDs have been successfully applied to in vivo tumor imaging in the second biological window, which afforded deep inner organ registration, dynamic tumor contrast, and fast tumor detection [199,200] (Fig. 6 ).

Two-photon and three-photon excitation microscopy using QDs as imaging contrast agents have also been developed. For each two-photon excitation, two low energy photons of the infrared light are absorbed to excite the QDs to generate a high-energy photon. The main advantage of two-photon microscopy is that the infrared excitation light would not cause significant tissue autofluorescence and possess a high tissue-penetration depth. QDs have a very high two-photon absorption cross section which is necessary for efficient two-photon excitation. QD-based two-photon excitation microscopy has been used for in vivo imaging studies such as blood vessel imaging [203], where a much higher imaging resolution is obtained for QDs than organic fluorophores. Similarly, QD-based three-photon excitation microscopy has been used for in vivo tumor imaging [215].

The recently developed self-illuminating QDs are superior probes for in vivo animal imaging. Both BRET and CRET-based self-illuminating QDs have been successfully applied to in vivo animal imaging [132,136–138]. Because these QD probes do not require light excitation, little imaging background was obtained and thus the imaging sensitivity could be significantly improved.

QDs possess several unique properties which make them versatile biosensors for the detection of various molecules ranging from metal ions and small molecules to large proteins and nucleic acids. Also, QDs could be used to monitor pH, temperature and oxygen. Both in vitro and in vivo detection using QD-based biosensors have been accomplished.

As an alternative to organic fluorophores, QDs have been extensively used as fluoro-tags for protein detection. The main principle for QD-based protein detection is similar to the traditional immunoassays [216–224]. Recently, several new protein detection strategies have been developed including DNA-based protein detection [225–227], doped QD-based multidimensional sensing platform for protein discrimination [228], dual-aptamer-based protein detection [229], electrochemiluminescent immunosensor [216,230], ratiometric QD protein sensor [231] et al.

Protease is an important type of biomarkers for many diseases. The upregualated expression level and activity of protease in several diseases could be detected with QD-based biosensors for disease diagnostics. FRET-based QD biosensor is the most popular tool to detect protease activities [144,232]. In this construct FRET acceptor is attached to the QDs through a peptide linker which is the substrate of protease (Fig. 7 ). Upon treatment with specific protease, the peptide linker is cleaved and the FRET signal is turned off. Several FRET acceptor have been used to construct the FRET pair with QDs, including fluorescent proteins [233], organic fluorophores [234–239], gold nanoparticles [240], quencher dyes [241,242] et al. Multiplexed detection of different protease has also been realized using multi-colored QD-based biosensors [243]. Similar to FRET, BRET was also adopted to detect protease activities, where bioluminescent proteins were used as BRET donors and QDs were used as BRET acceptors [133]. Recently new label-free strategies have been developed for high-sensitive detection of protease activities. For example, protease-triggered QD growth [244] and the inner filter effect of gold nanoparticles [245] were used to design protease-responsive biosensors to detect protease activities.

QDs have been widely applied to high-sensitive DNA and RNA detection. Because of their strong photoluminescence and multi-color properties, QDs could be used for multiplexed detection of different DNA targets simultaneously [246]. FRET-based strategy is the most popular one for DNA detection. For example, the target of interest could serve as a bridge to bring the QD-labeled probe and a fluorophore-labeled probe together and turn on FRET signal [247]. Alternatively, QD-tethered molecular beacons could be used to construct a FRET-based biosensor for DNA detection [248,249]. In some cases enzymes are used to amplify the target to improve the detection sensitivity [250–252]. In additional to FRET, the detection could be performed by bridging the QD-labeled DNA probe and magnetic nanoparticle-labeled DNA probe with the DNA target through hybridization. In this case the QDs are isolated by magnetic force and high detection sensitivity could be reached [253–255]. New label-free strategy has also been developed for high-sensitive DNA detection based on QD-doping [256].

QD-based biosensors have been used for the detection of many small biomolecules such as dopamine [257–259], ascorbic acid [260,261], amino acids [262], nucleotides [263–265], NADH [266], glucose [267,268] et al. FRET-based fluorescence microscopy and electrochemistry are most commonly used methods for small biomolecule detection using QD-based biosensors [257,258,263,267,269,270].

The optical properties of QDs exhibit temperature-dependent behavior and were used for temperature sensing in vitro [271]. High temperature sensitivity is realized via temperature-sensitive ligand molecule-induced QD transformation [272] or ratiometric sensing using doped QDs [273]. QDs have also been applied to intracellular pH sensing. For example, QDs could be conjugated with a FRET acceptor where the FRET efficiency is highly dependent on pH [274]. Alternatively, QDs could be conjugated with an electron acceptor that can quench QD photoluminescence in a pH-dependent manner [275]. A more direct approach relies on fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) [276]. The average PL lifetime of QDs is dependent on pH and thus could be used as an indicator for intracellular pH measurement. Oxygen sensing is another useful application of QDs. This sensing scheme is usually achieved by coupling the QDs with a FRET acceptor molecule (e.g. porphyrin, coenzyme Q) that could react with oxygen and transform to a state that would alter FRET efficiency [277–279].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a very promising cancer treatment method. It uses an excitation light and a photosensitizer to initiate apoptosis of cancer cells. The photosensitizer can be activated by light and then transfers its triplet state energy to nearby oxygen molecules to form reactive singlet oxygen (1O2) species, which can kill cancer cells. QD-photosensitizer conjugates have been constructed for cancer therapy [280–283]. In this construct QD serves as an antenna to absorb light and transfer the energy to the closely associated photosensitizer via resonance energy transfer to initiate singlet oxygen production [281,284,285]. The broad absorption spectrum of QDs would provide flexibility to excite the photosensitizer within a wide wavelength range. Also, because of the large two-photon absorption cross section of QDs, the QD-photosensitizer conjugates have been successfully applied to two-photon photodynamic therapy [286], which can hardly be realized by photosensitizer along.

Photothermal therapy (PTT) is an emerging technique for cancer treatment. It converts electromagnetic radiation to heat to kill cancer cells. Although a variety of nanomaterials such as gold nanoparticles and graphene have been used as PTT agents, the use of QDs for PTT has not been demonstrated until very recently. It is shown that CdTe and CdSe QDs could efficiently convert light energy into heat upon laser irradiation and the PTT application of these QDs was demonstrated in a tumor model with efficient tumor growth inhibition effect [287–290].

Simultaneous cancer diagnostics and therapy (theranostics) has emerged as a promising technology toward personalized medicine [291]. Along with their superior optical properties for bioimaging, QDs have been coupled with various drug carriers for targeted delivery of anticancer drugs for cancer theranostics. The most common drugs used for this purpose are doxorubicin, cisplatin, and siRNA. A variety of strategies have been developed to construct QD-drug nanocomposite. For siRNA delivery, the most straightforward method is directly absorbing the negatively charged siRNA onto the positively charged QD surface (e.g. amine-terminated) through electrostatic interactions [186,292,293] (Fig. 8 ). These QD-siRNA complexes exhibit high drug delivery efficiency and excellent gene silencing effect. Another common and universal strategy is to encapsulate the drugs and QDs into polymer micelles such as biodegradable PLGA polymers [294–296], pH-responsive amphiphilic copolymers [297], and mesoporous nanospheres [298]. Additionally, biorecognition molecules are tethered onto the surface of these nanocomplex to achieve targeted imaging and drug delivery [299–301]. In general, these nanocomplex are internalized via endocytosis and localize in late endosome and lysosomes where the low pH in these compartments would trigger drug release into cytosol [302,303]. Another interesting strategy for drug delivery is to use nucleic acid molecules as drug carriers for Dox delivery. Dox can efficiently incorporate into the nucleic acids duplex and be transported into the cells. The nucleic acids-based drug carriers have been demonstrated for RNA aptamers [304], DNA origami [305], and self-assembled DNA-nanoparticle hybrid nanostructures [306].

As better QDs probes have been designed and generated during the past several years, they are getting closer and closer to clinical applications in the near future. The impacts of QDs on immunoassay, diagnostics, and molecular pathology have already emerged recently. We believe that more robust and reproducible conjugation methods for QD probe production are a key point for their commercialization and widespread use. Additionally, the discovery of non-traditional QDs will pave the way for the in vivo clinical use of QDs.
